message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To investigate rates of adherence to the UK’s test, trace and isolate system over time.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Time series of cross-sectional online surveys.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Data were collected between 2 March and 5 August 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>42,127 responses from 31,787 people living in the UK, aged 16 years or over, are presented (21 survey waves, n≈2,000 per wave).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Identification of the key symptoms of COVID-19 (cough, high temperature / fever, and loss of sense of smell or taste), self-reported adherence to self-isolation if symptomatic, requesting an antigen test if symptomatic, intention to share details of close contacts, self-reported adherence to quarantine if alerted that you had been in contact with a confirmed COVID-19 case.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Only 48.9% of participants (95% CI 48.2% to 49.7%) identified key symptoms of COVID-19. Self-reported adherence to test, trace and isolate behaviours was low (self-isolation 18.2%, 95% CI 16.4% to 19.9%; requesting an antigen test 11.9%, 95% CI 10.1% to 13.8%; intention to share details of close contacts 76.1%, 95% CI 75.4% to 76.8%; quarantining 10.9%, 95% CI 7.8% to 13.9%) and largely stable over time. By contrast, intention to adhere to protective measures was much higher. Non-adherence was associated with: men, younger age groups, having a dependent child in the household, lower socioeconomic grade, greater hardship during the pandemic, and working in a key sector.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Practical support and financial reimbursement is likely to improve adherence. Targeting messaging and policies to men, younger age groups, and key workers may also be necessary.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>WHAT IS ALREADY KNOWN ON THIS TOPIC</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Test, trace and isolate systems are one of the cornerstones of COVID-19 recovery strategy.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The success of the test, trace and isolation system depends on adherence to isolating if symptomatic, getting a test if symptomatic, passing on details of close contacts if infection is confirmed, and quarantining of contacts.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Rates of adherence to test, trace and isolate behaviours in the UK need to be systematically investigated.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>WHAT THIS STUDY ADDS</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Self-reported adherence to test, trace and isolate behaviours is low; intention to carry out these behaviours is much higher.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Identification of COVID-19 symptoms is also low.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Practical support and financial reimbursement are likely to improve adherence to test, trace and isolate behaviours.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Adherence to the test, trace and isolate system: results from a time series of 21 nationally representative surveys in the UK (the COVID-19 Rapid Survey of Adherence to Interventions and Responses [CORSAIR] study)
